hints:
Tense: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple** (for negation we use do/does + not).
Explanation: the conditional part describes a possible situation, so the verb "come" is placed in Present Simple for the pronoun "you".
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*

a1.don't come:
Tense: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + base verb**.
Explanation: the result in First Conditional is expressed through will + the base form of the verb "be".
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a1.didn't come:
✅ "don't come" is correct because in the if-clause of First Conditional for the pronoun "you" we use Present Simple of the verb "come".
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a1.hadn't come:
❌ "didn't come" — Past Simple. In First Conditional, Present Simple is needed in the if-clause.
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a1.won't come:
❌ "hadn't come" — Past Perfect. For a possible condition in the present, we use Present Simple.
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*

a2.will be:
❌ "won't come" contains will, but we use will only in the main clause, not after if.
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a2.would be:
✅ "will be" is correct because the result of First Conditional is formed as will + the base form of the verb "be".
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a2.would have been:
❌ "would be" — the model of Second Conditional with would. We need will for a real future situation.
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*
a2.am:
❌ "would have been" belongs to Third Conditional (would have). In First Conditional, we use will + V1.
Example: *If you don't come to my birthday party tonight, I will be very upset. I want you to come!*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would + base verb**.
Explanation: in the second conditional, the result is expressed through would + the base form of the verb "say".
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!* 
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **if + had + V3** (or **had not + V3** for negation).
Explanation: the conditional part describes an unreal past result, so Past Perfect of "tell" is needed.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*

a1.wouldn't say:
✅ «wouldn't say» is correct because in the Second Conditional the result is expressed through would + the base form «say».
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a1.won't say:
❌ «won't say» uses will, which is characteristic of the First Conditional. Here, would is needed.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a1.wouldn't have said:
❌ «wouldn't have said» — Third Conditional (would have). In the Second Conditional we take would + V1.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a1.don't say:
❌ «don't say» — Present Simple. The result of the Second Conditional is formed using would.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*

a2.hadn't told:
✅ «hadn't told» is correct because in the if-clause of the Third Conditional for the pronoun «you» the form **had + V3** (or had not + V3) is needed.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a2.didn't tell:
❌ «didn't tell» — Past Simple. The conditional part of the Third Conditional requires **had + V3** to show the previous action.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a2.don't tell:
❌ «don't tell» — Present Simple. The Third Conditional describes the past and requires **had + V3**.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*
a2.wouldn't tell:
❌ «wouldn't tell» contains would, which is the form for the main clause. In the if-clause, **had + V3** should be used.
Example: *I wouldn't say Emma’s 29 years old if you hadn't told me. She looks much older! Thank God you told me!*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple** (for negation we use do/does + not).
Explanation: the conditional part describes a possible situation, so the verb "do" is placed in Present Simple for the pronoun «he».
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + subject + base verb** in an interrogative sentence.
Explanation: in the question of the result, will needs to be used before the subject and the base form «talk».
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*

a1.does:
✅ «does» is correct because in the if-clause of the First Conditional for the pronoun «he» we use Present Simple of the verb «do».
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a1.did:
❌ «did» — Past Simple. In the First Conditional, Present Simple is needed in the if-clause.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a1.had done:
❌ «had done» — Past Perfect. For a possible condition in the present, we use Present Simple.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a1.will do:
❌ «will do» contains will, but we only use will in the main clause, not after if.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*

a2.will you talk:
✅ «will you talk» is correct because in the interrogative form of the First Conditional we place will before the subject «pronoun «you»» and use the base form «talk».
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a2.would you talk:
❌ «would you talk» — Second Conditional with would. For a probable future question, will is needed.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a2.would you have talked:
❌ «would you have talked» — Third Conditional (would have). In First Conditional, we form questions with will.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*
a2.do you talk:
❌ «do you talk» — Present Simple. The result question of First Conditional requires will + subject.
Example: *I don’t think he’ll phone you but if he does, will you talk to him?*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + base verb**.
Explanation: the result in First Conditional is expressed through will + the base form of the verb «lose».
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple** (for negation we use do/does + not).
Explanation: the conditional part describes a possible situation, so the verb «stick» is in Present Simple for the pronoun «you».
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*

a1.will lose:
✅ «will lose» is correct because the result of First Conditional is formed as will + base form of the verb «lose».
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a1.would lose:
❌ «would lose» — model of Second Conditional with would. We need will for a real future situation.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a1.would have lost:
❌ «would have lost» belongs to Third Conditional (would have). In First Conditional, we use will + V1.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a1.lose:
❌ «lose» — Present Simple. The result of First Conditional must contain will.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*

a2.stick:
✅ «stick» is correct because in the if-clause of First Conditional for the pronoun «you» we use Present Simple of the verb «stick».
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a2.stuck:
❌ «stuck» — Past Simple. In First Conditional, the if-clause requires Present Simple.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a2.had stuck:
❌ «had stuck» — Past Perfect. For a possible condition in the present, we use Present Simple.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*
a2.are sticking:
❌ «are sticking» — Continuous form, and First Conditional requires simple Present Simple.
Example: *You will lose weight if you stick to the diet the doctor has told you to follow.*

hints:
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **if + had + V3** (or **had not + V3** for negation).
Explanation: the conditional part describes an unreal past result, so we need Past Perfect of «study».
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*

a1.had studied:
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **would have + V3**.
Explanation: the main part shows the result in the past, so we use would have + the third form of the verb «pass».
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a1.studied:
✅ «had studied» is correct because in the if-clause of Third Conditional for the pronoun «I» we need the form **had + V3** (or had not + V3).
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a1.study:
❌ «studied» — Past Simple. The conditional part of Third Conditional requires **had + V3** to show the previous action.
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a1.would study:
❌ "would study" contains would, and this is the form for the main clause. In the if-clause, **had + V3** should be used.
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*

a2.would have passed:
✅ "would have passed" is correct because the result of the Third Conditional is constructed as would have + V3 for the pronoun "I".
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a2.would pass:
❌ "would pass" — Second Conditional (would + V1) for present/future hypotheses. Here, would have + V3 is needed.
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a2.will pass:
❌ "will pass" — this is the model of the First Conditional with will. We need the form would have + V3 for the past result.
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*
a2.passed:
❌ "passed" — Past Simple. In the main clause of the Third Conditional, would have + V3 should be used.
Example: *If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam. Now it’s too late! I got a D!*

hints:
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **would + base verb**.
Explanation: in the second conditional, the result is expressed through would + the base form of the verb "eat".
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
Time: Second Conditional.
Formula: **if + Past Simple** (for to be, we use were with all persons).
Explanation: the conditional part describes an unreal present situation, so the verb "be" is put in Past Simple / were.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*

a1.would eat:
✅ "would eat" is correct because in the Second Conditional, the result is expressed through would + the base form "eat".
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.will eat:
❌ "will eat" uses will, which is characteristic of the First Conditional. Here, would is needed.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.would have eaten:
❌ "would have eaten" — Third Conditional (would have). In the Second Conditional, we take would + V1.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.eat:
❌ "eat" — Present Simple. The result of the Second Conditional is formed using would.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.were:
✅ "were" is correct because in the Second Conditional for the verb to be after if, we use were with all persons.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.was:
❌ "was" looks grammatically correct, but in a hypothetical if-sentence, we need were even with I/he/she/it.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.had been:
❌ "had been" — Past Perfect. For the present unreal condition, Past Simple (were) is needed.
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*
a1.am:
❌ "am" — Present Simple. The second conditional requires Past Simple (were).
Example: *I would eat more healthily if I were you.*

hints:
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **if + had + V3** (or **had not + V3** for negation).
Explanation: the conditional part describes an unreal past result, so Past Perfect of "go" is needed.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **would not have + V3** (wouldn't have + V3).
Explanation: the negative result is formed through would not have + the third form of the verb "meet".
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*

a1.hadn't gone:
✅ "hadn't gone" is correct because in the if-clause of the Third Conditional for the pronoun "I", the form **had + V3** (or had not + V3) is needed.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a1.didn't go:
❌ "didn't go" — Past Simple. The conditional part of the Third Conditional requires **had + V3** to show the previous action.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a1.don't go:
❌ «don't go» — Present Simple. The third conditional describes the past and requires **had + V3**.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a1.wouldn't go:
❌ «wouldn't go» contains would, which is the form for the main clause. In the if-clause, we should use **had + V3**.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*

a2.would never have met:
✅ «would never have met» is correct because the negation of the third conditional is formed as would not have + V3.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a2.would never meet:
❌ Incorrect form.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a2.will never meet:
❌ «will never meet» — this is Future/First Conditional with will not. For a past result, we need would not have + V3.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*
a2.never met:
❌ «never met» — Past Simple. The third conditional requires would not have + V3.
Example: *We’ve been married for 10 years. If I hadn't gone to that party, I would never have met him.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will + base verb**.
Explanation: the result in First Conditional is expressed through will + the base form of the verb «fail».
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple** (for negation we use do/does + not).
Explanation: the conditional part describes a possible situation, so the verb «study» is put in Present Simple for the pronoun «you».
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*

a1.will fail:
✅ «will fail» is correct because the result of First Conditional is formed as will + the base form of the verb «fail».
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a1.would fail:
❌ «would fail» — model Second Conditional with would. We need will for a real future situation.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a1.would have failed:
❌ «would have failed» belongs to Third Conditional (would have). In First Conditional, we use will + V1.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a1.fail:
❌ «fail» — Present Simple. The result of First Conditional must contain will.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*

a2.don't study:
✅ «don't study» is correct because in the if-clause of First Conditional for the pronoun «you» we use Present Simple of the verb «study».
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a2.didn't study:
❌ «didn't study» — Past Simple. In First Conditional, the if-clause needs Present Simple.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a2.hadn't studied:
❌ «hadn't studied» — Past Perfect. For a possible condition in the present, we use Present Simple.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*
a2.won't study:
❌ «won't study» contains will, but we use will only in the main clause, not after if.
Example: *You will fail your final exams next week if you don't study harder.*

hints:
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **if + had + V3** (or **had not + V3** for negation).
Explanation: the conditional part describes an unreal past result, so we need Past Perfect of «know».
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
Time: Third Conditional.
Formula: **would have + V3**.
Explanation: the main part shows the result in the past, so we use would have + the third form of the verb «tell».
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*

a1.had known:
✅ "had known" is correct because in the if-clause of the Third Conditional for the pronoun "I" the form **had + V3** is needed.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a1.knew:
❌ "knew" — Past Simple. The conditional part of the Third Conditional requires **had + V3** to show a previous action.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a1.know:
❌ "know" — Present Simple. The Third Conditional describes the past and requires **had + V3**.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a1.would know:
❌ "would know" contains would, which is a form for the main clause. In the if-clause, **had + V3** should be used.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*

a2.would have told:
✅ "would have told" is correct because the result of the Third Conditional is built as would have + V3 for the pronoun "I".
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a2.would tell:
❌ "would tell" — Second Conditional (would + V1) for present/future hypotheses. Here, would have + V3 is needed.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a2.will tell:
❌ "will tell" — this is the model of the First Conditional with will. We need the form would have + V3 for the past result.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*
a2.told:
❌ "told" — Past Simple. In the main part of the Third Conditional, would have + V3 should be used.
Example: *If I had known about this, I would have told you, but I promise I didn’t know Robert was coming to the party.*

hints:
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **will not / won't + base verb**.
Explanation: negation in the main part of the First Conditional is formed through will not (won't) + base form "buy".
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
Time: First Conditional.
Formula: **if + Present Simple** (for negation we use do/does + not).
Explanation: the conditional part describes a possible situation, so the verb "be" is in Present Simple for the pronoun "they".
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*

a1.won't buy:
✅ "won't buy" is correct because the negation in the main part of the First Conditional is built as will not / won't + base form "buy".
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a1.wouldn't buy:
❌ "wouldn't buy" — Second Conditional with would. For a real future situation, will not / won't is needed.
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a1.wouldn't have bought:
❌ "wouldn't have bought" — Third Conditional (would not have). In the First Conditional, we use the simple form will not + V1.
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a1.doesn't buy:
❌ "doesn't buy" — Present Simple. The result of the First Conditional must contain will (not).
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*

a2.aren't:
✅ "aren't" is correct because in the if-clause of the First Conditional for the pronoun "they" we use the Present Simple of the verb "be".
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a2.weren't:
❌ "weren't" — Past Simple. In the First Conditional, the if-clause needs Present Simple.
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a2.won't be:
❌ "won't be" contains will, but will is only used in the main part, not after if.
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
a2.haven't been:
❌ «haven't been» — Present Perfect. The conditional part of the First Conditional requires simple Present Simple.
Example: *Richard won't buy these sneakers if they aren't on sale. He will only get them for an affordable price.*
